humanities_role_explanation: Evaluates domain knowledge of historical Chinese clothing (Hanfu) across dynasties through visual question answering and provides a task for creative transcreation of traditional designs into modern wearable clothing.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Combines images of Hanfu attire with natural language questions (for visual question answering) and text prompts (for transcreation/image translation).
analytical_tasks_explanation: Factual recognition of historical clothing features (sleeves, collar, jin, period, type) via multiple-choice VQA, and cross-temporal cultural adaptation (transforming traditional garments into modern, wearable designs while preserving cultural elements).

general_purpose_benchmark.note: It is a domain-specific cultural benchmark focusing specifically on Chinese Hanfu clothing across dynasties.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluated MiniCPM-V 2.6, Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct, InternVL2.5, GPT-4o, and Doubao-1.5-V.

llm_is_primary_system.note: The primary systems being evaluated are Vision-Language Models (VLMs) on VQA and image transcreation tasks.

arts_humanities_data.note: Focuses on Visual Arts and Performing Arts (costume/fashion design), History (dynastic styles), and Conservation (cultural heritage).

quantitative_metrics.note: Uses Accuracy for VQA tasks, and 5-point human evaluation metrics for transcreation (visual-change, semantic-equivalence, naturalness, modern-adaptability, cultural-inheritance, attractiveness).

english_language.note: The paper is written in English, though some dataset and prompt elements are bilingual (Chinese and English).

summary: Hanfu-Bench is a manually curated multimodal benchmark designed to evaluate vision-language models on cross-temporal Chinese cultural understanding and adaptation. It features two core tasks: cultural visual understanding via single- and multi-image VQA, and cultural image transcreation, which assesses the models' ability to transform traditional Hanfu designs into modern attire while preserving traditional cultural elements.
key_findings: Closed VLMs perform comparably to non-expert humans on cultural visual understanding but lag by roughly 10% behind experts, while open VLMs perform worse than non-experts. On the image transcreation task, the best-performing model (Stable-Diffusion-2-1-base guided by GPT-4o) achieved a success rate of only 42% according to multi-faceted human evaluation.
